title: The Author
wikitext:
[]: The Author             Quote Post by Felipe » Thu Mar 14, 2013 11:19 am Something I often struggle with is just how much validity can I credit to the idea of the Auteur of any given oeuvre, if at all. I do acknowledge that this is ultimately a personal decision, but I think the discussion is really valid since Evangelion lends itself so well to this topic. I'm not a radical in any of the two sides of this subject, so I'll give my opinions: The author does not have complete control of the meaning of the medium - ultimately, we have to make our own decisions about say, what exactly happened in Instrumentality, if it is a good thing, etc, because every element of storytelling will, to some extent, be subjective. I do not take it to the extreme, however: certain interpretations are clearly wrong, because often (even in Eva) the authorial intent is quite obvious (I'd even argue that the message portrayed in Eva is really clear). But let's say we reach a conclusion about a determined piece of art, which is contradicted later by its author - what do we do? This has happened quite a few times in Eva, if you read some of Anno's statements about the show. Do we change our minds about the subject? Or do we disregard the author completely, and stick to our feelings in face of evidence showing that we weren't supposed to see things the way we did? Specially with mythological information, like the ones we only have through secondary sources like the PS2 games. Sure, Anno wrote the CI, but does that even matter? If, say, the Ancestral Race is never even hinted towards in the show, do we have to take it into consideration when thinking about it? I personally am of the opinion that it is a bit of a failure of the author of any given piece of art to have to explain it through any other kind of medium other than the piece of art itself. To me, if I have to write encyclopedia-like entries in a game to make you understand what was going on on the previous TV show that I wrote, I simply failed to convey what I meant, budgetary restraints or not - or I didn't care to add it to the show at the time, and neither should you. But that's, just, like my opinion, man.

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Thu Mar 14, 2013 12:37 pm The author's intent is irrelevant, only what's on screen or on the page matters. The author's interpretation of the work is valid but no more valid than an independent interpretation that's based in evidence from the text. If you're looking to "prove" anything about a work of fiction or establish that one point of view is correct you're barking up the wrong tree. Art is not mere simulation. That said, the paratext and supplemental material in the video games, etc. isn't a sign of a failure on the creator's part. All of that is exactly what it appears to be- fanservice. Most of the nitty gritty details about the function of this or that technobabble or the First Ancestral Race or what have you is just fanservice and doesn't really add or detract from the themes of the show and film.

[]:             Quote Post by Lavinius » Thu Mar 14, 2013 12:57 pm The author's intent is a vital part of the art. Without it the work is merely thoughtless nothing. Similiarly, the art requires the work, without it nothing has been created to be seen. The Good Sir here has written a commentary on End of Evangelion in which he differs from the intent of Anno-sama. Anno-sama (or whoever else is responsible for the bit in question) still is truly correct, and his idea is the true version of Eva. The Good Sir has rather created his own art, with his own intent and work, drawing inspiration from Eva. As for the Classified Information, if it is from Anno-sama then it is true. He just didn't find it important enough to explicitly state in the series, though it was in his mind.

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Thu Mar 14, 2013 1:06 pm We are incapable of determining the author's intent. We can only see what we see. Searching for a "true" meaning imposes an arbitrary limit on the work, which must be allowed to speak for itself. Yes, I create my own art by interpreting the work. That's all anyone does, some people just prefer to pretend otherwise and search for an illusory basis of authenticity.

[]:             Quote Post by Lavinius » Thu Mar 14, 2013 1:13 pm Unless, of course, the author tells us of his intent. To be sure, an exact understanding of the intent is not possible, but a reasonable approximation is. To use the old Platonic example, you might as well say that no one has ever seen a circle because every circle in the world isn't precisely a set of extentionless points the same exact distance from another extentionless point.

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Thu Mar 14, 2013 1:16 pm If we're gonna discuss this about Evangelion, then I shall start by scoffing at any argument around any side of the author's intent , and instead point at the authors' intents. TV shows like this are a collaborative production, from concept to marketing. And while this is needed to just get the production off the ground and get the show on the road, it can result in "too many cooks in the kitchen" in any one department if it's not properly delegated by the producers or studio heads. So this can quickly become a case of "Who's message is this anyway?" While Anno oversaw the show's direction, we can blame most of everything on him. (For whatever reason critics blame directors, while everyone from directors to gaffers complain about the producer. Then there are cases where everyone from producers to gaffers blame the studio heads, in which case the producer is also about to look for a job with another studio. It's weird.) There are some situations where others might be to blame. Anno pretty much handed the writing for Episode 4 to the other people on his writing staff. Sure, we can still blame Anno if something went terribly wrong, but then the discussion would also have to include whether or not those writers were able to convey their intentions apart from Anno's assistance. So I think we should start by asking "Was the author's intent portrayed as he wanted it?" followed by the question, "Which author?" then, "Was the director right in trusting that author?" For example: "Did the Island Arc of Hideaki Anno's Nadia: The Secret of Blue Water correctly portray the author's intent?" "Which author?" "Shinji Higuchi." "Yeah, but the intent was flawed and stupid." "Was Anno right in trusting Higuchi?" "Creatively speaking, no. But I blame the network heads at NHK for asking GIANAX for more episodes. That's what caused that whole fiasco in the first place. If Anno didn't do that, people's jobs would be at stake." ...Or maybe it would be better to just ask if the ideas present in the works themselves were correctly portrayed rather than asking about a one-and-only "author," seeing as how we already have too many cooks in the kitchen.

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Thu Mar 14, 2013 1:36 pm Lavinius wrote: Unless, of course, the author tells us of his intent. To be sure, an exact understanding of the intent is not possible, but a reasonable approximation is. To use the old Platonic example, you might as well say that no circle in the world is actually a circle because it isn't precisely a set of extentionless points the same exact distance from another extentionless point.

[]:             Quote Post by Lavinius » Thu Mar 14, 2013 1:41 pm Well, if they don't understand it (in the sense of knowing what their intentions were) then it isn't art. Unintentional meanings are not part of the art, they merely arise in others from viewing the art- they are the intent of an as-of-yet uncreated art.

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Thu Mar 14, 2013 1:55 pm Lavinius wrote: Well, if they don't understand it (in the sense of knowing what their intentions were) then it isn't art. Unintentional meanings are not part of the art, they merely arise in others from viewing the art- they are the intent of an as-of-yet uncreated art.

[]:             Quote Post by Felipe » Thu Mar 14, 2013 2:31 pm Chuckman wrote: We are incapable of determining the author's intent. We can only see what we see. Searching for a "true" meaning imposes an arbitrary limit on the work, which must be allowed to speak for itself.

[]:             Quote Post by ObsessiveMathsFreak » Thu Mar 14, 2013 6:30 pm My own personal view is that Neon Genesis Evangelion is Hideaki Anno's lucid dream, told through the medium of a Gainax television production. Our job is to interpret the work and its meanings as best we can through the barriers of language, emotion, and the inevitable fallibility of all artistry. On the matter of the Confidential Information files being "needed" to understand Evangelion: This applies only to the science-fiction narrative in which the story is told, and does not even apply absolutely as much of the CI information is in fact hinted at in the show. But the real message of Evangelion is emotional and psychological, and I think this message was carried across in the original production. The series was an exploration of the human psyche and condition. Anno's original statement (made at the beginning of the production), is the most relevant here Hideaki Anno wrote: It is a production where my only thought was to burn my feelings into film. I know my behavior was thoughtless, troublesome, and arrogant. But I tried. I don't know what the result will be. That is because within me, the story is not yet finished. I don't know what will happen to Shinji, Misato or Rei. I don't know where life will take them. Because I don't know where life is taking the staff of the production. I feel that I am being irresponsible. But... But it's only natural that we should synchronize ourselves with the world within the production.

[]:             Quote Post by Paranoid » Sat Mar 16, 2013 12:11 pm Reading this thread, I'm reminded of when I went to see a Rothko (y'know, the guy who painted colored squares) exhibit in a museum about five years ago. Upon one person in the tour complaining that he "didn't get it", the museum guide explained that he had to try and find his own meaning through how this art made him feel. I couldn't help but laugh. Why? Because she was standing under a huge quote by Rothko on the wall, which said that all of his art has specific, objective meaning and that if you don't get it then you simply don't understand his art. I can't find the exact quote, but wiki does say that he denied being an abstractionist. The whole issue of objective vs. subjective meaning is fairly complex, but here's my take on it: For me, it's all about consistency. As long as new information presented by the original author doesn't conflict with anything we know, then it's canon. However, when the author says something that doesn't agree with your interpretation of the art, or when you deduce something based on your interpretation that doesn't seem to have been intended by the author (and so long as it doesn't conflict with established facts), it's up to you to analyze and judge. I disagree with the idea that the "author knows best". If you take into account the fact that Eva is all about the emotions and relationships of the characters, and the fact that Anno himself was supposedly distraught at the time and in large part wrote the show as an extension of himself, it seems to me that it is reasonable to assume that he did not fully understand the characters. Oh, he certainly empathized with them, but that doesn't imply full understanding. After all, if people (and especially distraught people) understood everything about their own emotions, we wouldn't need psychology to begin with. To illustrate my point, I offer this: Imagine that you wrote your exact happenings and feelings in a diary every day. Now, imagine that someone took this diary and made a movie script out of it. The character would be very realistic - after all, it's based on a real character with real thoughts and emotions. How well you knew yourself at the time hardly enters into this. It's certainly possible that other people might see this movie and understand things about you that you yourself never realized, and that doesn't make their conclusions any less valid, nor does this mean that self-portraits (either paintings or literary works) aren't art.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Sun Mar 17, 2013 7:45 am Chuckman wrote: The author's intent is irrelevant, only what's on screen or on the page matters. The author's interpretation of the work is valid but no more valid than an independent interpretation that's based in evidence from the text.

[]:             Quote Post by pwhodges » Sun Mar 17, 2013 8:22 am Bagheera wrote: Trendy, but false. Authorial intent matters for many sorts of analyses, but is irrelevant for others. Privileging the one over the other gives a distorted view of the work as a whole (we've moved from one distortion to another, currently; the death of author concept must die itself before we can formulate a balanced approach to interacting with any given work).

[]:             Quote Post by Elect G-Max » Thu Mar 28, 2013 6:53 pm What is this thread actually ABOUT?

[]:             Quote Post by pwhodges » Thu Mar 28, 2013 6:59 pm The title and the posts should make it clear enough, or you could read up about authorial intent , for instance.

If, say, the Ancestral Race is never even hinted towards in the show, do we have to take it into consideration when thinking about it? I personally am of the opinion that it is a bit of a failure of the author of any given piece of art to have to explain it through any other kind of medium other than the piece of art itself. To me, if I have to write encyclopedia-like entries in a game to make you understand what was going on on the previous TV show that I wrote, I simply failed to convey what I meant, budgetary restraints or not - or I didn't care to add it to the show at the time, and neither should you. Article: The Author
 of when I went to see a Rothko (y'know, the guy who painted colored squares) exhibit in a museum about five years ago. Upon one person in the tour complaining that he "didn't get it", the museum guide explained that he had to try and find his own meaning through how this art made him feel. I couldn't help but laugh. Why? Because she was standing under a huge quote by Rothko on the wall, which said that all of his art has specific, objective meaning and that if you don't get it then you simply don't understand his art. I can't find the exact quote, but wiki does say that he denied being an abstractionist. The whole issue of objective vs. subjective meaning is fairly complex, but here's my take on it: For me, it's all about consistency. As long as new information presented by the original author doesn't conflict with anything we know, then it's canon. However, when the author says something that doesn't agree with your interpretation of the art, or when you deduce something based on your interpretation that doesn't seem to have been intended by the author (and so long as it doesn't conflict with established facts), it's up to you to analyze and judge. I disagree with the idea that the "author knows best". If you take into account the fact that Eva is all about the emotions and relationships of the characters, and the fact that Anno himself was supposedly distraught at the time and in large part wrote the show as an extension of himself, it seems to me that it is reasonable to assume that he did not fully understand the characters. Oh, he certainly empathized with them, but that doesn't imply full understanding. After all, if people (and especially distraught people) understood everything about their own emotions, we wouldn't need psychology to begin with. To illustrate my point, I offer this: Imagine that you wrote your exact happenings and feelings in a diary every day. Now, imagine that someone took this diary and made a movie script out of it. The character would be very realistic - after all, it's based on a real character with real thoughts and emotions. How well you knew yourself at the time hardly enters into this. It's certainly possible that other people might see this movie and understand things about you that you yourself never realized, and that doesn't make their conclusions any less valid, nor does this mean that self-portraits (either paintings or literary works) aren't art.
